text: The surgeon uses a tip-up grasper to elevate the recto-sigma junction, jiggling the peritoneum to identify the junction between the mesorectum and retroperitoneum. An incision is made at this junction to enter the avascular plane, preserving nerves and pushing down a bridging vessel. The left ureter is identified as the dissection progresses medially to laterally. The anatomical landmarks include the transverse colon, splenic flexure, descending colon, sigmoid colon, duodenal-jejunal junction, IMV, IMA, left kidney, and pancreas.

The video shows the anatomy of a thin patient with an obstructing sigmoid cancer. The transverse colon, splenic flexure, and descending colon are visible, along with the duodenal-jejunal junction and the IMV passing below the duodenum. The IMA is seen entering the fatty para-aortic tissue. The surgeon uses a tip-up grasper to hold tissue and circumferentially dissect the IMA distal to its aortic takeoff. Firefly imaging is used to identify the ureter encased by the tumor. The IMV is divided near the duodenal-jejunal junction with a one-centimeter cuff.

The surgeon uses a tip-up grasper to elevate tissue and begins circumferential dissection of the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA) distal to its takeoff from the aorta. The left ureter is visible, squiggling in the retroperitoneum, while areolar tissue is incised. Firefly imaging is used to identify the ureter with ICG. The surgeon strips peritoneum and lymph nodes from the vessel, maintaining a cuff of tissue. The inferior mesenteric vein (IMV) is divided near the duodenal-jejunal junction, with the pancreas visible above.

The surgeon performs a medial to lateral dissection, jiggling the peritoneum to identify the junction between the yellowish mesorectal fat and the paler retroperitoneal fat. An incision is made at this junction, exposing areolar tissue and entering the pre-sacral space. The dissection continues below the IMA, peeling it off the aorta while carefully pushing down the left ureter, hypogastric nerves, and retroperitoneal fat using passive movements with the left hand.

The surgeon performs a medial to lateral dissection, identifying the junction between the yellowish fat of the mesorectum and the palish retroperitoneal fat. An incision is made, exposing areolar tissue and entering the presacral space. The dissection proceeds below the IMA, peeling it off the aorta while identifying the left ureter and hypogastric nerves. The surgeon uses a sub-IMV approach, tenting the inferior mesenteric vein anteriorly, and incising posteriorly to access the avascular plane. The dissection creates a T-shaped configuration of the IMA, with the aorta below and the left colic and superior hormonal arteries branching off.

The surgeon uses a sub-IMV approach, tenting up the inferior mesenteric vein by lifting the mesenteric fat into a twelve o'clock orientation. The IMV is clearly visible, and an incision is made immediately posterior to it, pushing down the retroperitoneal attachments to enter the avascular plane. The IMV is elevated, and critical structures are pushed down, connecting the sub-IMV incision to the supra-IMA incision. This dissection creates a T-shaped configuration with the aorta below, the left colic artery heading up, and the superior hormonal artery heading down, with the IMA in the center.

The surgeon tents the recto-sigma junction anteriorly to identify the bare area above the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA). Using the left hand, the surgeon incises into the retroperitoneal space, revealing the left ureter beneath gonadal vessels and retroperitoneal vasculature. The supra-IMA approach is utilized to expose the ureter, with visible anatomical landmarks including the duodenum and aorta, and the IMA taking off from the aorta.

The surgeon uses cautery to tent up the inferior mesenteric vein (IMV) anteriorly, making an incision immediately posterior to the IMV. The areolar tissue is incised, and the mesentery is swept anteriorly, entering into the avascular plane. The thin tissue planes are visible, and the IMV is elevated off the duodenum, revealing the pancreas at the lower border where the IMV inserts. A window is created around the IMV for skeletonization and division.

The surgeon uses cautery to make an incision immediately posterior to the inferior mesenteric vein (IMV), sweeping the mesentery anteriorly to enter the avascular plane. The thin tissue planes are carefully navigated to avoid entering the mesentery or kidney. The IMV is elevated off the duodenum, revealing the pancreas at the lower border, where a window is created to skeletonize and divide the IMV.
